planting.note: Sow direct in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; seeds benefit from cold stratification over winter and germinate in spring — common sneezeweed is native to moist prairies, stream banks, and low meadows across most of North America.

directions: Sow seeds direct in fall on the soil surface (light aids germination) in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; seeds cold-stratify naturally over winter and germinate in early spring — common sneezeweed thrives in consistently moist to wet soils and is poorly suited to dry conditions. Helenium autumnale is a tall, clump-forming native perennial producing winged stems with decurrent leaf bases and large, yellow, daisy-like flowers with distinctively reflexed ray petals fanning backward from a prominent rounded, yellow-brown disk from August through October — one of the most important late-season pollinator plants in North America. It is exceptionally attractive to native bumblebees, sweat bees, and specialist Helenium-foraging bees; the pollen-rich disk flowers support enormous populations of small native bees during the critical late-season period before frost. Divide established clumps every 3–4 years in spring to maintain vigor and increase bloom; plants in rich, moist soils may need staking if they exceed 4 feet.

planting.note: Sow direct around last frost in dry, lean, well-drained soil in full sun; bitterweed is a native annual or short-lived perennial of disturbed roadsides, dry fields, and open sandy ground across the Gulf Coast and South — WARNING: all parts are toxic to livestock.

directions: Sow direct around the last frost date in dry, lean, sandy to loamy, well-drained soil in full sun; bitterweed germinates readily in warm soil and grows quickly in poor, dry conditions where richer-soil plants fail — it requires excellent drainage and is intolerant of consistently wet or waterlogged soils. Helenium amarum is a native annual or short-lived perennial of the southeastern United States, ranging from the Gulf Coast states north through the Mid-Atlantic; it produces very fine, thread-like, linear leaves on bushy, much-branched stems and masses of small, bright-yellow, daisy-like flowers with reflexed ray petals around a rounded, yellow-green disk from June through frost. It is an important late-season nectar source for native bees, wasps, and butterflies, particularly on roadsides and disturbed sites where few other natives thrive; it self-sows freely and forms dense colonies in dry, open ground. CAUTION: all plant parts contain bitter sesquiterpene lactones (helenin) that are toxic to cattle, horses, and sheep if consumed in quantity — do not plant in or adjacent to livestock pastures.

planting.note: Sow direct in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade for cold stratification and spring germination; purple-headed sneezeweed is native to moist meadows, stream margins, and wet roadsides across eastern North America.

directions: Sow seeds direct in fall on the soil surface (seeds are light-sensitive and germinate better with some light) in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; purple-headed sneezeweed thrives in consistently moist to wet soils and is well suited to rain gardens, stream banks, and low wet meadows. Helenium flexuosum is closely related to H. autumnale but is distinguished by its striking, deep purple-brown to chocolate-colored disk (center cone), which creates a vivid color contrast against the bright yellow reflexed ray petals — this dark, globe-shaped disk is its most diagnostic feature and makes it one of the most visually dramatic native sneezweeds. It blooms from June through September across eastern North America from the Midwest to the Atlantic Coast and south to Florida; it is an outstanding pollinator plant supporting native bees, bumble bees, and specialist helenium-foraging native sweat bees. Like H. autumnale, the winged, decurrent stems are a helpful identification feature; divide clumps every 3 years in spring to maintain vigor.

planting.note: Sow direct in fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Virginia mountain mint spreads by rhizomes to form colonies and is one of the most exceptional native pollinator plants in the eastern United States, attracting an extraordinary diversity of native bees.

directions: Sow direct in fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Virginia mountain mint can also be propagated from stem cuttings or division in spring — division is often preferred as seeds can be slow to establish. Pycnanthemum virginianum is a strongly aromatic native perennial of moist prairies, meadows, and stream margins across the eastern United States; it produces dense, flat-topped clusters of tiny, white to pale-lavender, two-lipped flowers with purple spots from July through September that are covered almost constantly with an extraordinary diversity of native bees, wasps, butterflies, and beetles, making it one of the single highest-rated native pollinator plants in North American horticulture. The strongly minty, intensely aromatic foliage deters deer and rabbits while remaining highly attractive to beneficial insects; it spreads moderately by short rhizomes to form manageable colonies. Virginia mountain mint is a plant that belongs in every eastern native plant garden — few plants support as many pollinator species per square foot.

planting.note: Sow direct in fall in well-drained, moderately moist, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; narrowleaf mountain mint is more drought-tolerant than P. virginianum and adapts well to average, dry to moist garden soils once established.

directions: Sow direct in fall in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; narrowleaf mountain mint is more adaptable to dry, average soils than P. virginianum and tolerates poor, sandy, or rocky conditions better — once established it is moderately drought-tolerant. Pycnanthemum tenuifolium is a fine-textured, strongly aromatic native perennial of the eastern United States distinguished by its very narrow, thread-like (tenuifolium = narrow-leaved) leaves that give it an airy, delicate appearance compared to its broader-leafed relatives; it produces an abundance of small, flat-topped clusters of tiny white flowers with faint purple spots from July through September. It is consistently rated among the top three native plants for pollinator diversity in eastern North America — the flowers simultaneously support hundreds of individual native bee species, wasps, flies, and small butterflies over a six-to-eight-week bloom period. Spreads by rhizomes to form dense, weed-suppressing colonies; the minty-fragrant foliage is strongly deer- and rabbit-resistant. Excellent in native meadow plantings, dry to moist borders, and roadsides.

planting.note: Sow direct in fall in well-drained, average to lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; lyre-leaf sage is one of the few native sages tolerant of part shade and self-sows freely in lawns, woodland edges, and open disturbed areas across the Southeast.

directions: Sow direct in fall in well-drained, average to lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; lyre-leaf sage self-sows prolifically once established and can naturalize in lawns, under deciduous trees, and along woodland edges — it is one of the most shade-tolerant native sages in North America, which is remarkable given that most Salvia species require full sun. Salvia lyrata is a native biennial to short-lived perennial of the eastern United States producing a basal rosette of deeply lobed, lyre-shaped (lyrate) leaves that are often flushed with attractive purple-maroon coloring in winter and early spring; in its flowering year it sends up slender, upright stems bearing whorls of tubular, pale blue to blue-violet flowers from April through June that are visited by native bumblebees, small bees, and ruby-throated hummingbirds. The winter rosette is evergreen in mild climates and provides early-season ground cover; plants self-sow abundantly enough to persist as a self-perpetuating colony. It is particularly effective naturalized under deciduous oaks and in partly shaded lawn areas where few other native wildflowers will bloom.
